refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit reference to measurement/monitoring (telemetry) and site conditions to ensure the FR is verifiable and context-bound. High safety criticality tailoring requires that cycle time optimization does not compromise safety interlocks or operator control responsiveness.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure p95 cycle time across ≥100 representative load→travel→dump cycles under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Validate that measured p95 ≤ CT_target. Cross-check telemetry logs for consistency. Confirm no safety alerts or operational policy violations during measurement window.
sub_frs: FR-2.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing and minimal variance.
FR-2.2: The teleoperation system shall support remote cycle execution with latency and control responsiveness that does not degrade cycle time below target.
FR-2.3: The safety services shall enforce cycle-time-critical safety interlocks without introducing blocking delays that exceed acceptable thresholds.
FR-2.4: The NGHE solution shall collect and report cycle time telemetry (start, end, sub-phase timestamps) for p95 calculation and performance trending.

candidate_dps: DP-2a: Optimize vehicle hydraulic and control system response times; minimize mechanical friction and control loop latency; tune PID parameters for load, travel, dump sub-cycles.

DP-2b: Implement low-latency teleoperation network stack with QoS prioritization; reduce round-trip latency to <100ms for operator commands; use predictive control and local autonomy to mask network jitter.

DP-2c: Design safety interlocks as non-blocking, parallel-execution state machines; use hardware-accelerated safety logic to avoid software scheduling delays; pre-compute safety checks during idle phases.
DP-2d: Instrument vehicle and teleoperation systems with cycle-phase event logging; aggregate telemetry at NGHE solution level; compute p95 statistics in real-time and expose via Telemetry interface.
selected_dp.selected_dp: Integrated approach: Combine DP-2a (vehicle optimization), DP-2b (low-latency teleoperation), DP-2c (non-blocking safety), and DP-2d (telemetry instrumentation). Cycle time is a system-level emergent property; no single DP is sufficient. The selected DP is a coordinated design strategy across vehicle dynamics, network control, safety logic, and observability.

potential_sources: Vehicle hydraulic response time (DP-2a) is coupled to teleoperation latency (DP-2b): if network latency exceeds vehicle response time, operator cannot maintain smooth cycle rhythm.
Safety interlocks (DP-2c) may conflict with cycle time optimization (DP-2a): aggressive acceleration profiles may trigger safety thresholds; requires joint tuning.
Telemetry instrumentation (DP-2d) introduces measurement overhead; if logging is synchronous, it may block cycle execution and degrade measured cycle time.
Duty cycle variability and site conditions (load mass, terrain, ambient temperature) create parameter dependencies that are not fully decoupled across DPs.
coupling_check.notes: The coupling is expected and acceptable at this level because cycle time is inherently a system-level KPI. However, the couplings must be explicitly managed during detailed design. Recommend decomposition into independent sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.4) to isolate and manage each coupling at the next level.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: CT_target is achievable with current vehicle platform and network infrastructure. Risk: If CT_target is set below the physical limits of the vehicle or network, no DP can satisfy the FR.
Assumption: Duty cycle and site conditions are well-defined and stable. Risk: If conditions vary significantly (e.g., load mass ±50%), cycle time variance may exceed p95 target.
Assumption: Safety interlocks can be implemented with <10ms latency overhead. Risk: If safety logic is complex (e.g., multi-sensor fusion), latency may exceed budget and degrade cycle time.
Assumption: Telemetry collection does not introduce >5% cycle time overhead. Risk: If logging is synchronous or high-frequency, measurement artifact may mask true performance.
Assumption: Operator skill and tele-operator latency tolerance are consistent. Risk: Tele-operator fatigue or network congestion may introduce variability that inflates p95.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Decompose the selected DP into four independent sub-DPs aligned with FR-2.1 through FR-2.4. For each sub-DP, explicitly define coupling points and integration tests. Establish a cycle-time budget (e.g., 10% margin for safety, 5% for telemetry, 85% for vehicle execution) and allocate to each sub-system. Validate that CT_target is achievable before proceeding to detailed design.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system integration module responsible for coordinating vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services to achieve cycle time KPI. It owns the cycle-time budget allocation, telemetry aggregation, and performance monitoring. It does not own vehicle hardware, network infrastructure, or safety logic implementation; those are delegated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules respectively.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits cycle-phase timestamps (load_start, load_end, travel_start, travel_end, dump_start, dump_end) and computed p95 cycle time statistics to external monitoring/reporting systems. Consumed by Site Manager and Project Manager for KPI tracking.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives cycle execution commands (load, travel, dump) from tele-operator or autonomous planner. Tasking includes duty cycle parameters (load mass, travel distance, dump location) that inform cycle time prediction and safety checks.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety alerts from Safety Services module. If a safety alert is triggered during cycle execution, NGHE Solution must halt or abort the cycle and report the event via Telemetry.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution coordinates network connectivity between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. It monitors network latency and QoS to ensure teleoperation responsiveness does not degrade cycle time below target.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of cycle-time KPI because it is the only module with visibility across vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. Cycle time is an emergent property of the integrated system, not a property of any single sub-module. Containment is high because all cycle-time-relevant interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are already defined at the NGHE Solution level. Diffusion risk is low: cycle time logic is localized to NGHE Solution; sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for their own performance targets, not cycle time directly.

Vehicle module owns the load, travel, dump sub-cycle execution and hydraulic/control system optimization (DP-2a). However, Vehicle alone cannot achieve cycle time KPI because it depends on teleoperation latency and safety interlocks. If cycle time ownership is assigned to Vehicle, it will diffuse responsibility for network and safety performance, leading to finger-pointing and poor integration. Not recommended as primary owner.

Teleoperation module owns network latency and operator control responsiveness (DP-2b). However, Teleoperation cannot control vehicle hydraulic response time or safety interlock latency. Cycle time ownership at Teleoperation level would create false accountability and obscure vehicle/safety performance issues. Not recommended as primary owner.

Safety Services module owns safety interlock design and latency optimization (DP-2c). However, Safety Services is not responsible for vehicle dynamics or teleoperation performance. Assigning cycle time ownership to Safety Services would create confusion about safety vs. performance trade-offs and diffuse accountability. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Cycle time KPI is a system-level emergent property that requires coordination across vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. NGHE Solution is the only module with end-to-end visibility and authority to allocate cycle-time budgets, monitor performance, and trigger corrective actions. All four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are already defined at the NGHE Solution level, confirming that the module boundary is appropriate for this FR.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if too many cross-cutting concerns are assigned to it. Mitigation: Clearly define NGHE Solution responsibilities as coordination and monitoring only; delegate implementation of DP-2a, DP-2b, DP-2c, DP-2d to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Telemetry modules respectively.

Risk: Cycle time performance may be limited by external factors (site conditions, duty cycle variability, operator skill) that are outside NGHE Solution control. Mitigation: Establish clear assumptions about duty cycle and site conditions in the FR; define contingency plans if actual conditions deviate.

Risk: Telemetry interface may not provide sufficient granularity (e.g., sub-phase timestamps) to compute p95 accurately. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry interface schema to include load_start, load_end, travel_start, travel_end, dump_start, dump_end timestamps with <1ms precision.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may introduce blocking delays if safety checks are synchronous. Mitigation: Require Safety Services to implement non-blocking, parallel-execution safety logic (DP-2c); define maximum acceptable latency for SafetyAlert propagation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that NGHE Solution module boundary includes a dedicated cycle-time monitoring and budget allocation sub-component. Define explicit interfaces between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services for cycle-time-relevant parameters (e.g., target cycle time, allocated budget, measured performance). Establish a cycle-time control loop: measure → compare to target → adjust vehicle/teleoperation/safety parameters → re-measure. Document assumptions about duty cycle, site conditions, and operator skill in the FR.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP is a multi-faceted strategy (DP-2a + DP-2b + DP-2c + DP-2d) that addresses cycle time as a system-level property. However, the DP is not sufficiently specific or actionable at this level. The FR requires decomposition into atomic sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.4) before a single, well-defined DP can be selected for each. The current DP is a collection of design strategies, not a single coherent solution. Recommend ZIGZAG_DOWN to decompose FR into sub-FRs and select independent DPs for each.

diffusion_risks: DP-2a (vehicle optimization) is owned by Vehicle module; DP-2b (teleoperation latency) is owned by Teleoperation module; DP-2c (safety interlocks) is owned by Safety Services module; DP-2d (telemetry) is owned by NGHE Solution. This distribution is appropriate and avoids diffusion within any single module.

However, the integration of these four DPs is owned by NGHE Solution, which must coordinate cycle-time budgets and monitor end-to-end performance. This is a legitimate system-level responsibility and does not constitute diffusion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for cycle-time KPI ownership because it has visibility across all sub-systems and can enforce cycle-time budgets. The four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all defined at the NGHE Solution level, confirming that the module boundary is well-aligned with the DP.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable: measure p95 cycle time across ≥100 representative cycles under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Telemetry interface provides the necessary data (cycle-phase timestamps) to compute p95. Verification is feasible if duty cycle and site conditions are well-defined and stable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution with clear sub-allocations to Vehicle (FR-2.1), Teleoperation (FR-2.2), Safety Services (FR-2.3), and Telemetry (FR-2.4). Each sub-FR is assigned to a specific module, and ownership is clear. However, the allocation is conditional on successful decomposition and detailed design of each sub-FR.

recommended_revisions: Decompose the refined FR into four atomic sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.4) before proceeding to detailed design. Each sub-FR should be assigned to a specific module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, Telemetry) with a clear, independent DP.

Establish a cycle-time budget (e.g., 85% for vehicle execution, 5% for teleoperation latency, 5% for safety interlocks, 5% for telemetry overhead) and allocate to each sub-system. Validate that the sum of allocated budgets is ≤ CT_target.

Define duty cycle and site conditions explicitly in the FR (e.g., load mass range, travel distance, terrain type, ambient temperature). Establish acceptance criteria for cycle time performance under these conditions.

Specify Telemetry interface schema to include cycle-phase timestamps (load_start, load_end, travel_start, travel_end, dump_start, dump_end) with <1ms precision. Confirm that Telemetry interface is sufficient for p95 calculation.

Establish a cycle-time control loop at the NGHE Solution level: measure → compare to target → adjust vehicle/teleoperation/safety parameters → re-measure. Define feedback mechanisms and corrective action triggers.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and verifiable, but the selected DP is a multi-faceted strategy that requires decomposition into independent sub-DPs. The FR-DP coupling is acceptable but not tight enough for detailed design. The IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate, but the module boundary must be clarified to distinguish between cycle-time coordination (NGHE Solution responsibility) and cycle-time implementation (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services responsibility). Recommend decomposing the FR into four atomic sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.4) and selecting independent DPs for each before proceeding to detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing and minimal variance, achieving a target sub-cycle time allocation (e.g., 85% of CT_target).

FR-2.2: The teleoperation system shall support remote cycle execution with latency and control responsiveness that does not degrade cycle time below target, achieving a target latency allocation (e.g., <5% of CT_target).

FR-2.3: The safety services shall enforce cycle-time-critical safety interlocks without introducing blocking delays that exceed acceptable thresholds, achieving a target safety latency allocation (e.g., <5% of CT_target).
FR-2.4: The NGHE solution shall collect and report cycle time telemetry (start, end, sub-phase timestamps) for p95 calculation and performance trending, with <5% measurement overhead.

revise.why: The refined FR is correct but should be explicitly decomposed into four sub-FRs to enable independent DP selection and module assignment at the next level. The current FR is a system-level KPI that is best understood as a composition of four independent sub-requirements.

assumptions: CT_target is achievable with current vehicle platform and network infrastructure.
Duty cycle and site conditions are well-defined and stable (load mass, travel distance, terrain, ambient temperature).
Safety interlocks can be implemented with <5% latency overhead relative to CT_target.
Telemetry collection introduces <5% cycle time overhead.
Operator skill and tele-operator latency tolerance are consistent across measurement window.
p95 is the appropriate statistical measure for cycle time KPI (vs. mean, median, or max).
open_risks: Risk: CT_target may be set below the physical limits of the vehicle or network. Mitigation: Validate CT_target feasibility during detailed design.
Risk: Duty cycle variability and site conditions may exceed assumed ranges, inflating p95. Mitigation: Establish contingency plans and acceptance criteria for off-nominal conditions.
Risk: Safety interlocks may introduce latency that exceeds allocated budget. Mitigation: Implement non-blocking, parallel-execution safety logic; conduct latency analysis during detailed design.
Risk: Telemetry instrumentation may introduce measurement artifact that masks true performance. Mitigation: Validate telemetry overhead through testing; use asynchronous logging if necessary.
Risk: Tele-operator fatigue or network congestion may introduce variability that inflates p95. Mitigation: Establish operator training and network QoS requirements.
evidence_needed: Validation that CT_target is achievable with current vehicle platform and network infrastructure (e.g., simulation, prototype testing).
Definition of duty cycle and site conditions (load mass range, travel distance, terrain type, ambient temperature).
Latency analysis for safety interlocks (e.g., worst-case latency, jitter, blocking delays).
Telemetry overhead measurement (e.g., CPU usage, memory footprint, impact on cycle time).
Operator training and skill assessment (e.g., tele-operator latency tolerance, fatigue effects).
Network QoS analysis (e.g., latency, jitter, packet loss under various load conditions).
p95 statistical analysis (e.g., distribution of cycle times, confidence intervals, sensitivity to outliers).
